title: Open Mic

jokes.text: It's your safe. You have the combination.
jokes.setup: Roland asks Ronnie for the combination to the safe as if it's a normal request she'd fulfill.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie flatly points out it's his safe — the absurdity of the ask is exposed instantly.
jokes.explanation: A tight, clean deadpan opener that immediately establishes Ronnie's no-nonsense dynamic with Roland. The economy is excellent — one line does all the work. Loses points for being a fairly standard 'you're an idiot' setup.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, I had to call a locksmith last week. Wendy locked me out of the house.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, 'locked me out.' By accident, she says.

jokes.text: I used a guy this weekend. Yeah, Gwen locked me out. By accident.
jokes.setup: Roland volunteers that he has a locksmith's number from recent experience.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that his wife locked him out, followed by the defensive 'by accident' that suggests it might not have been.
jokes.explanation: The tiny 'by accident' is where the joke lives — it's a small character reveal about the Roland/Gwen marriage dynamic delivered with perfect comic timing. Very Roland-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: The pregnancy itself is already surprising enough.

jokes.text: Your wife's pregnant in her 40's. How many surprises do you need?
jokes.setup: Roland explains he and Jocelyn want to keep the baby's sex a surprise as a family tradition.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie cuts through the sentiment by pointing out that a 40-something pregnancy is already surprise enough.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharper jokes. The structure is clean: Roland's sincere sentiment sets up a perfectly timed deflation from Ronnie. The reframe ('the pregnancy itself is the surprise') is genuinely witty and very much Ronnie's voice. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, let me think. The combination is either my birthday or Gloria Estefan's birthday.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would the safe combination be Gloria Estefan's birthday?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you never know. It's good to have options.

jokes.text: It's either my birthday, or Gloria Estefan's.
jokes.setup: Roland needs to crack the safe combination himself and thinks through what it could be.
jokes.punchline: The fact that Gloria Estefan's birthday is a plausible safe combination for Roland — equal in weight to his own birthday.
jokes.explanation: This is a gem of character comedy. The specificity of Gloria Estefan — not a generic celebrity, but a particular cheesy touchstone — perfectly captures Roland's sensibility. The casual equivalence between his own birthday and Estefan's is absurdly funny. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: Bonjour, how are you?

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: I'm doing très bien, gracias.

jokes.text: Mucho Dias, mes amis.
jokes.setup: Roland enters mid-conversation with a greeting.
jokes.punchline: He mashes Spanish and French together into a nonsensical hybrid greeting, delivered with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland — confidently incorrect. The joke works because he's not trying to be funny, he's just Roland. The linguistic collision is mild but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It actually was Gloria Estefan's birthday!

jokes.text: Okay, turns out it's Gloria's, September 1, 57.
jokes.setup: Roland tried the safe combination — it was either his birthday or Gloria Estefan's.
jokes.punchline: It was Gloria's. Of course it was.
jokes.explanation: The callback lands better than the original setup. Confirming it was Gloria's birthday (and not Roland's) is the perfect escalation — it says everything about Roland without saying anything. The specificity of the date adds delightful verisimilitude. Rewatch bonus because you anticipate it differently knowing the payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: What the hell is this? Did someone put a ghost picture on my desk?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a sonogram, Ronnie.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's an ultrasound. Someone's pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Well why didn't they just say so instead of leaving ghost pictures all over my desk?

jokes.text: Who put a picture of a ghost on my desk?
jokes.setup: Roland placed the sonogram envelope on Moira's desk, hoping to keep it secret.
jokes.punchline: Moira, with characteristic dramatic obliviousness, thinks the sonogram image is a ghost.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira line. Only Moira would look at a sonogram and see a ghost. The line is hysterical on its own, but it also perfectly characterizes her theatricality and disconnect from ordinary life. High character integration score — this joke could not come from any other character on the show.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I know the baby's gender. I'm carrying emotional cargo now.

jokes.text: Roland, why should I be the only one encumbered with this emotional cargo?
jokes.setup: Moira has accidentally seen the sonogram label revealing the baby's sex, and Roland wants her to keep quiet.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames keeping a secret as being 'encumbered with emotional cargo' — hilariously melodramatic language for a minor inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Another strong Moira vocabulary joke — 'emotional cargo' for knowing a baby's sex is exactly her register. The character integration is near-perfect, though the joke is slightly weaker than her 'ghost' line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, might we confabulate for a moment? Privately?

jokes.text: Oh, Ronnie, Ronnie, might you and I confabulate for a moment in the back room? No, Moira, I'm not falling for that one.
jokes.setup: Moira, bursting with the secret, tries to lure Ronnie to the back room to tell her.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie is wise to Moira's tricks — implying this is a recurring pattern — and refuses flatly.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Ronnie knowing Moira's exact game — 'I'm not falling for that one' implies a history of Moira using pretexts to rope people in. Fun dynamic between these two characters, and 'confabulate' is a perfect Moira word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, might I have a word with you? In private?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Oh, here we go. You want something.

jokes.text: Moira successfully lures Ronnie to the back room by claiming it's 'a salacious bit of Council-related news' — cut to Ronnie being told the baby secret.
jokes.setup: Ronnie refuses to go to the back room. Moira pivots and offers 'salacious Council news' as bait.
jokes.punchline: The implication (via scene cut) that Ronnie fell for it anyway — the bait worked, and Ronnie is now stuck with the secret too.
jokes.explanation: The implied joke — Ronnie's resolve caved immediately when 'salacious Council news' was dangled — is a fun character beat. Works as a callback and mild escalation on the prior joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, I have something rather salacious and council-related to discuss with you.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Salacious and council-related? Those are contradictory. Town council business is the opposite of salacious.

jokes.text: I'm gonna say it, we've been open for 3 hours now, and not a single customer's walked through that door.
jokes.setup: Patrick states the obvious problem with the store after a long pause.
jokes.punchline: The dramatic build-up ('I'm gonna say it') for a crushingly mundane business failure.
jokes.explanation: A functional setup for the B-plot. The 'I'm gonna say it' framing is mildly funny by itself — treating a business observation like a controversial opinion. Competent but not distinguished.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, maybe the storm warning was retroactive.

jokes.text: Well, I mean, I heard that there was a storm warning for today, so. / How do you explain yesterday? / Maybe the storm warning was for yesterday. / So then how do you explain today? / Okay, I don't have all the answers.
jokes.setup: David deflects Patrick's concern about no customers with a storm warning excuse.
jokes.punchline: Patrick points out the excuse doesn't cover yesterday either, David shifts the storm, Patrick catches that too — ending with David's defeated 'I don't have all the answers.'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best comedic exchange. The escalating logic trap — David invents a storm warning, Patrick closes the loophole for yesterday, David shifts the storm to yesterday, Patrick closes that loophole — is brilliantly structured. David's final 'I don't have all the answers' is a perfect character line: pompous but immediately deflated. The structure is tight and the character integration is excellent for both. This is professional-grade comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, we haven't had a customer in three days. What is going on?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, David. People just aren't coming in.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not really an explanation. Have you tried doing anything to attract customers? Advertising? A promotion?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know what? Sure. Yes. I did all of that.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You did?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No. I'm just going to say yes to everything you say now because this conversation is exhausting.

jokes.text: Um, have we checked to see if the door is unlocked?
jokes.setup: They've been brainstorming reasons why no customers came in.
jokes.punchline: David suggests the most basic possible explanation — the door might just be locked — as if this is a serious analytical insight.
jokes.explanation: David's obliviousness played earnestly — he's genuinely offering this as a contribution. Works because it's delivered straight, not as self-deprecation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We should find better ways to engage with the community.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, let's just find any way to engage with the community.

jokes.text: You know why people are always in the café? Because they feel at home there, it feels like... comfortable. Okay, are you saying that I don't make people feel at home? Um...
jokes.setup: Patrick floats the theory that the café's comfort draws customers. David asks if Patrick is saying HE is the problem.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'Um...' — a classic non-answer that is itself the answer — confirms David is the problem without Patrick having to say it.
jokes.explanation: The comedic pause where Patrick says 'Um...' instead of denying it is perfectly timed. It's honest, relationship-specific comedy — Patrick genuinely can't lie to David, but also can't say 'yes you're the problem.' The silence lands.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, people showed up for the free alcohol, not because they believe in us or our business.

jokes.text: Yes! It was opening day, and there was booze. And people are drunks.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks David if he remembers their opening day, which was successful.
jokes.punchline: David's explanation for opening day success: 'there was booze. And people are drunks.' Delivered with total conviction.
jokes.explanation: The bluntness of 'people are drunks' delivered as David's unironic business insight is very funny. Classic David Rose — cynical, accurate, and presented as wisdom. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We should host an open mic night at the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... that's a lovely idea, Patrick. Really. I'm very open to new experiences and community engagement. However, if you do that, I will quit.

jokes.text: What if we hosted an open mic night? / I am open to suggestions.
jokes.setup: David said he's 'open to suggestions.' Patrick pitches an open mic night.
jokes.punchline: David's reflexive 'I am open to suggestions' — repeating his exact prior line — signals his complete horror at the idea while technically not shutting it down.
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant: David's line callbacks his own words from seconds ago, using repetition as deflection. It's a sophisticated comedy technique — the repeated phrase reveals his appalled reaction without any new words. Very David, very quotable. This is writing-room-level joke craft.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I was in an improv troupe in high school.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: *visibly recoils*

jokes.text: So there was a point at which you, and some of your fellow amateur singer songwriters would gather and perform poetry and songs? / Yeah. / For one another? / Mhm. That's right. / Oh god.
jokes.setup: Patrick describes his high school open mic experience with genuine warmth.
jokes.punchline: David methodically recaps each detail back to Patrick in increasingly horrified terms — 'for one another?' — before the simple 'Oh god.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character comedy sequences. David's interrogation-style recap — breaking the experience into its most mortifying components — is pitch-perfect. 'For one another?' as a standalone question, dripping with contempt, is brilliant. 'Oh god' as the verdict is devastating in its simplicity. Pure David Rose character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Worst case, we get people in the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Watching improv is worse than business failure.

jokes.text: The occasional improv troupe would stop by. / I'm um... I'm feeling kind of ill.
jokes.setup: David is already horrified by the open mic concept.
jokes.punchline: Patrick mentions improv as an additional feature. David's physical revulsion — 'I'm feeling kind of ill' — is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the previous joke. Improv is the one thing more cringe than open mic to someone like David. The physical illness reaction is funny because it's so disproportionate and yet completely understandable for the character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What about the comment cards? Don't you read those?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, I don't read those.

jokes.text: Okay, no, worst case scenario, I watch improv.
jokes.setup: Patrick says 'worst case scenario, we get some people in the store.'
jokes.punchline: David corrects him: the actual worst case scenario is being forced to watch improv.
jokes.explanation: This is the best single-line joke in the episode. The structure is perfect: Patrick offers a reassurance ('worst case scenario'), David reframes what the actual worst case is — not commercial failure but being subjected to improv. It's a precise inversion, hysterically quotable, and completely specific to David's character. Economy is nearly perfect. This is the 8+ anchor for the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Most of these are just from bitter people who have nothing better to do than leave passive aggressive comments.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Although, I have to say, a lot of these are from my mother.

jokes.text: This place needs a tonne of work, like top to tail. / I think what you mean to say is proposed improvements? / No, I mean like full-blown things that need fixing.
jokes.setup: Alexis presents her assessment of the motel's problems.
jokes.punchline: Stevie tries to soften 'needs fixing' into 'proposed improvements.' Alexis refuses the spin — 'No, I mean like full-blown things that need fixing.'
jokes.explanation: Funny because Alexis, usually the one to soften bad news with spin, is here the blunt one while Stevie (in manager mode) tries corporate-speak. The role reversal is character-specific and amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you guys have a website?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that is so cute. It's like, very 1997 of you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is that... is that the little construction guy with the orange cones?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because that is literally the saddest thing I've ever seen.

jokes.text: They're just a placebo effect for people who feel the need to complain.
jokes.setup: Alexis points out that they don't read comment cards.
jokes.punchline: Johnny reframes ignoring complaints as a feature, not a bug — the cards are a 'placebo effect.'
jokes.explanation: A nice managerial self-delusion joke. 'Placebo effect' is an interesting word choice that slightly elevates what could be a generic 'we ignore complaints' beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, who talks like that? So aggressive.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That was me.

jokes.text: Those cards are written by embittered people with way too much time on their hands. / A lot of these are from Mom.
jokes.setup: Johnny dismisses comment cards as written by embittered complainers.
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals a lot of the cards are from Moira — confirming Johnny's assessment while also making it much worse.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is flawless: Johnny characterizes the complainers (embittered, too much time), and Alexis' single line reveals those complainers are his own wife. It validates and devastates simultaneously. Excellent economy — the punchline is four words. High quotability. Very Schitt's Creek in its warm-but-savage family comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This automated voice is so robotic and awful. Like, how hard is it to sound like an actual human being?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That was me. I had strep throat.

jokes.text: We have a website. / No. I looked it up, and it's just like a cartoon of a construction worker holding a sign that says 'we'll be right back'. / It's under construction.
jokes.setup: Alexis says the motel doesn't have a website. Stevie and Johnny defensively claim they do.
jokes.punchline: Alexis describes what's actually there — a construction worker placeholder — and Johnny's non-defense 'It's under construction' which has been true presumably for years.
jokes.explanation: The image of the cartoon construction worker placeholder is an instantly recognizable and funny bit of visual storytelling. Johnny's straight-faced 'It's under construction' as a defense of what is obviously permanent neglect lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I prefer 'image consultant and lifestyle architect.'

jokes.text: The phone booking system is confusing, and the woman's voice was aggressive and off-putting. / I had strep at the time.
jokes.setup: Alexis critiques the phone booking system's aggressive recorded voice.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's defensive 'I had strep at the time' — confirming she IS the aggressive voice and that this was considered good enough to keep.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The reveal that Stevie recorded the phone system herself (and while sick, no less) is both unexpected and completely believable for this motel's chaotic management style. 'I had strep at the time' as a defense — not an apology, not a promise to fix it — is perfect Stevie character comedy. Very economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what is Step 1 of your plan?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The presentation itself was Step 1. There was no actual plan yet.

jokes.text: I actually prefer to be called a freelance brand invigorator.
jokes.setup: Johnny wonders if Alexis should be called a 'publicist.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis insists on the made-up title 'freelance brand invigorator' with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Classic Alexis — she has invented an elaborate title for a role she's essentially making up on the spot. 'Brand invigorator' is perfectly Alexis: vague, impressive-sounding, meaningless. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, in my family, we have a tradition of keeping the baby's gender a secret until birth.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The origin story is that my great-great-grandmother was accused of witchcraft for guessing a baby's gender correctly.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: She was almost tried, so now we never guess. It's safer that way.

jokes.text: Step 1 of the plan was this presentation, and we nailed it. So, as your new VP of Marketing and Communications, I'd say we're off to a great start. / Yeah. / I'll pass.
jokes.setup: Alexis reveals she's already declared herself VP of Marketing and Communications, claiming the presentation as Step 1.
jokes.punchline: Johnny tentatively agrees; Stevie flatly says 'I'll pass' — a single two-word veto.
jokes.explanation: The structure is satisfying: Alexis' self-appointment and triumph contrasted with Stevie's two-word veto. The brevity of 'I'll pass' against Alexis' elaborate self-congratulation is a clean comedic juxtaposition. Very Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Double, double toil and trouble; fire burn and caldron bubble.

jokes.text: Salem, Massachusetts, actually. / Yeah, his great-great-great great-grandmother accurately guessed the sex of her daughter's baby, and then was put on trial for witchcraft, so, you would see why. / Toil and trouble.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains the Schitt family tradition of not revealing the baby's sex goes 'way back.'
jokes.punchline: It goes back to Salem witch trials — a woman who guessed a baby's sex was tried for witchcraft. Moira's response: 'Toil and trouble' (misquoting Macbeth).
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the family tradition backstory to Salem witch trials is delightfully absurd and specific. Moira's 'Toil and trouble' (the Macbeth misquote — it's actually 'Double, double toil and trouble') is perfect: theatrical, slightly wrong, and completely her. Original premise with good character payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So Moira, have you thought about what you're going to name the baby? I'm thinking if it's a boy, maybe something strong like...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, I can see exactly where you're going with this, and the answer is no.

jokes.text: I mean, you must just wanna shout out 'it's a boy!' Or, 'it's a girl!' / Is it a girl? / Valiant attempt, Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn pressures Moira empathetically, then asks directly.
jokes.punchline: Moira sees through the 'empathy bait' and calmly identifies it as an attempt — 'Valiant attempt, Jocelyn.'
jokes.explanation: 'Valiant attempt, Jocelyn' is an excellent line — condescending but not unkind, which is Moira's specific register. The joke is in Moira's calm recognition of the manipulation while praising the effort. Very quotable and very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, if my ancestor hadn't been burned at the stake for witchcraft, we wouldn't be in this predicament now.

jokes.text: Stupid witch!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn, embarrassed at being caught trying to extract the secret, says she wouldn't want to break Schitt family tradition.
jokes.punchline: Immediately calls Moira a 'stupid witch' under her breath (or possibly aloud).
jokes.explanation: The callback to witchcraft makes 'stupid witch' work on two levels: it's a frustrated insult AND a reference to the Salem backstory. Jocelyn's character — usually sweet and accommodating — cursing out Moira is funny in itself. Economy is perfect: two words, huge reaction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What are you holding?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's an acoustic guitar. It's a stringed instrument that produces sound through vibration of the strings, which resonates within the hollow body of the guitar. Unlike an electric guitar, it doesn't require amplification.

jokes.text: Veronica! / She just have forgotten her handbag. / I'm sorry, you were talking about something?
jokes.setup: Moira, struggling to contain the secret, suddenly shouts 'Veronica!' — apparently at a passerby.
jokes.punchline: She covers it as the woman forgetting her handbag, then innocently asks Jocelyn what they were discussing — as if she definitely didn't almost blurt out the baby's name.
jokes.explanation: This is a lovely physical-comedy-meets-character-comedy beat. Moira almost blurts out what is presumably the baby's name (or a gender-telling name), pivots with a cover story, and resets with faux-innocence. The audience gets to piece together what happened. Very Moira, and the rewatch bonus is real — you catch more the second time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I'm worried about your performance tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why? I've been practicing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, but there's a 60% chance you're going to mess up, and I'm not willing to risk our relationship on those odds.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: 60%? That seems high.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It is high. It's very high. Which is why I'm concerned.

jokes.text: Someone took a long coffee break. / Oh, I'm sorry, did I miss the evening rush? / Uh, yes, one customer came in, who was asking for directions.
jokes.setup: David returns from a long absence. Patrick sarcastically notes his 'long coffee break.'
jokes.punchline: David's sarcastic 'did I miss the evening rush?' is answered genuinely and painfully: one customer, asking for directions.
jokes.explanation: The joke lives in the specificity: not zero customers, but one — who was asking for directions. The precise nature of this failure is funnier than a general 'nobody came in' beat. David's sarcasm gets literalized.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What if I wore my fringed vest?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'what if'? You're not wearing it.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: But what if I did?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Then you'd look like you were going to a Renaissance fair.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What if I didn't wear anything under it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, no.

jokes.text: This is called an acoustic guitar.
jokes.setup: David reacts with visible alarm to Patrick bringing in a guitar and asks what it is.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's flat, literal answer — treating David like someone who has never seen a guitar — delivered with complete innocence.
jokes.explanation: The economy here is spectacular — Patrick deadpans an obvious statement because David's alarm reaction implied he'd never seen a guitar before. The gap between David's horror and Patrick's matter-of-fact response is perfectly timed. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not a real thing.

jokes.text: So then you will be... playing your acoustic guitar / in front of people then, in public. / And singing a song. / And singing a song.
jokes.setup: Patrick explains he'll be performing at the open mic night he's hosting.
jokes.punchline: David's slow, horrified recap — 'in front of people then, in public' — treating this as an escalating catastrophe, mirroring the earlier 'for one another?' joke structure.
jokes.explanation: Echoes the earlier interrogation-style recap structure (joke #16) but now it's personal — Patrick is going to perform. The phrase 'in front of people then, in public' as a revelation is funny because Patrick is simply confirming normal concert behavior as if it's outrageous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, Dad, take one step left.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Like this?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, more left.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How's this?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Keep going left.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm going to walk right out of the room.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: I was thinking about singing an original song. / An original song? / But then I thought you know, it's probably best just to stick with a classic. / Yeah. Classic.
jokes.setup: Patrick reveals he'll perform an original song — David's horror spikes — then walks it back to a classic.
jokes.punchline: David's 'Yeah. Classic.' — the relief of dodging the original song bullet, delivered as if commenting on the obvious right choice.
jokes.explanation: The rewatch bonus applies strongly here — on rewatch, you know Patrick is lying, which makes David's relieved 'Yeah. Classic.' heartbreakingly ironic. In the moment it's a mild laugh; in retrospect it's a much richer joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So the angle is that Stevie doesn't care about her appearance, which is like, crazy liberating? And then people see that and they're like, 'Oh my God, I don't have to care either.' It's actually genius marketing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Did you just call me ugly?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No! I called you... effortlessly indifferent. Which is way better.

jokes.text: I think that's cool. / David, you're gonna be fine.
jokes.setup: David sarcastically claims it's 'not scary or embarrassing' for your boyfriend to serenade you in public.
jokes.punchline: After David's tortured 'I think that's cool,' Patrick simply says 'you're gonna be fine' — treating David's existential dread as a minor manageable issue.
jokes.explanation: A warm beat rather than a big laugh — Patrick's calm reassurance against David's panic is more charming than comedic. Functional but not standout.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Stevie's Place? That sounds like a funeral home.

jokes.text: if you are confident, to put yourself and our relationship at risk like that, then I am... 87% behind you.
jokes.setup: David is framing his 'support' for Patrick's open mic performance.
jokes.punchline: 87% — the specificity of the number is funnier than a round number would be. He's withholding a precise 13% of support.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 87% is the joke — it implies David has actually calculated his support percentage, and the missing 13% is doing real work. A classic David Rose absurdist-precision gag. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We could call it the Stevie Budd Motel.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, wait—no, that's not... I didn't mean...

jokes.text: Good to know. So now the only question is, do I wear my fringed vest? Or more importantly, do I wear anything under it?
jokes.setup: Patrick accepts David's conditional support and pivots to wardrobe.
jokes.punchline: 'More importantly, do I wear anything under it?' — the escalation from fringed vest (already alarming for David) to potentially shirtless fringed vest.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's cheerful obliviousness to David's suffering is perfectly captured here — he's genuinely excited and is now making it worse with wardrobe questions. 'More importantly' as the pivot to shirtlessness is a good escalation beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why are we focusing on solo shots?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, because your group photos are... they're not reading.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Your posture is off.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Your eyes are dead.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: And you have the energy of a man who's just been told his cat has passed.

jokes.text: Yes, uh, Stevie, Mr. Rose, the motivation here is two small-town friends welcoming guests to their humble motel.
jokes.setup: Alexis directs the photo shoot with the intensity of a professional photographer.
jokes.punchline: The 'motivation' language from professional photography applied to a motel photo shoot in a small town by Alexis is inherently absurd.
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny — Alexis playing professional photographer/director is in-character, but this particular beat doesn't have a sharp punchline. Sets up subsequent jokes in the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you must know, I did peek at the ultrasound, but I was distracted by the technician's accent, and now everyone's asking me and I'm starting to doubt what I saw.

jokes.text: Oh, should I be holding a key? / No, Mr. Rose, unless you wanna look really, really corny. / Yeah, like really, really cheesy, and off-brand.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to contribute to the photo shoot concept.
jokes.punchline: Both Alexis and Ray pile on to explain why Johnny's idea is terrible, their enthusiastic agreement with each other making it worse for Johnny.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about Johnny being ganged up on. The double-correction is a bit predictable but works. 'Off-brand' from Alexis is an Alexis-specific word that lands.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I kept that secret so well, I hid it from myself.

jokes.text: If the camera was your guest, how would you greet her? / I don't know. / Um, welcome to the Schitt's Creek Motel. I'm Johnny Rose. / Um, yes, but no, don't use your mouth. / What the fuck does that mean?
jokes.setup: Alexis directs Johnny to 'talk to the guests with your face' and 'smile with your eyes.'
jokes.punchline: Johnny asks her to explain the instruction. She says don't use your mouth. Johnny: 'What the fuck does that mean?' — expressing the audience's own confusion.
jokes.explanation: Johnny swearing is always good — it punctures his usual buttoned-up dad persona. The frustration is earned, and 'what the fuck does that mean' is the audience surrogate line. Works well.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He put the combination note inside the locked safe

jokes.text: Okay, Johnny, what are we doing with our hands? / Alexis, I don't love you calling me Johnny.
jokes.setup: Alexis, in full director mode, critiques Johnny's hand positioning.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's only complaint is her calling him 'Johnny' rather than 'Dad' — his dignity is being eroded step by step and this is the one line he draws.
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat. Johnny has been completely pushed around in this shoot and his one pushback is about his name, not the larger indignities. The priority inversion is funny and very human.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I've locked the combination inside the safe.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's quite a blunder.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Massive understatement calling it just a 'blunder.'

jokes.text: Mr. Rose, would you mind taking one step to your left? / [pause] Okay now, three more steps to your left.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks Johnny to step to the left for the photo — seemingly to adjust framing.
jokes.punchline: The second instruction — three MORE steps to the left — implies she's removing him from the frame entirely. The visual escalation (Johnny shuffling further and further out of shot) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'one step' to 'three MORE steps' is perfectly structured physical comedy. The pause between instructions lets the audience realize Alexis is actively eliminating Johnny from the photo. Excellent structure and economy. The visual payoff (Johnny standing off to the side bewildered) would earn big laughs.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Stevie, you're going to look amazing. I mean, not in like a traditional sense, but in a way that's very you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It means you're going to look like yourself, which is great because you're very unique looking.

jokes.text: Pretty country girl who doesn't care about big city things like... dressing well, or how she looks.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains her vision for marketing the motel using Stevie as the 'hook.'
jokes.punchline: The description of Stevie's appeal is a backhanded compliment — she's appealing BECAUSE she doesn't care about appearance — delivered with complete sincerity by Alexis.
jokes.explanation: Alexis' oblivious compliment-insult is very in-character. The pause before 'dressing well, or how she looks' suggests Alexis is choosing her words carefully, making it funnier — she thinks she's being diplomatic.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, David. What if this actually works? What if people actually want what we're selling? Then I'm responsible for something. And if I'm responsible for something, people are going to expect things from me. And if people expect things from me, they're going to be disappointed when I inevitably let them down, and then they're going to leave.

jokes.text: We could rename the motel like, Stevie's Place. / Stevie's Place sounds like a shelter for battered men.
jokes.setup: Alexis floats the idea of renaming the motel after Stevie.
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately reframes 'Stevie's Place' as sounding like a shelter for battered men — a very specific, very dark image.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best one-liners. 'Stevie's Place sounds like a shelter for battered men' is original, unexpected, dark, and delivered with Stevie's perfect deadpan. The specificity — not just a refuge but specifically FOR BATTERED MEN — is what elevates this. Very quotable and very Stevie. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Look at this group photo, Ray. What do you think?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, you all look... half-normal.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Half-normal? That's supposed to be a compliment?

jokes.text: we could use your last name, and call it the Budd Inn. / And now that I'm saying that out loud, obviously we're not gonna do that.
jokes.setup: Alexis pivots from 'Stevie's Place' to using Stevie's last name — Budd — for the motel name.
jokes.punchline: Alexis starts to say 'Budd Inn' and immediately catches that it sounds like 'Buddy Inn' (or possibly something worse) and self-corrects in real time.
jokes.explanation: The self-correction 'and now that I'm saying that out loud, obviously we're not gonna do that' is funny because Alexis catches her own mistake mid-idea. Also a setup for the later 'Rosebud Motel' reveal and the 'Budd with two d's' joke. Rewatch bonus for the foreshadowing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't allow him to do anything. I was very much against this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, so you expressed your concerns?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I didn't say anything.

jokes.text: Solo? / Don't take it personally, Mr. Rose, it's probably because you didn't know what to do with your hands. Or maybe it's because your face looked like it tasted something sour. Or maybe... / Thank you, Ray, I get it.
jokes.setup: Johnny reacts to being told he'll be cut from the motel photo in favor of a solo Stevie shot.
jokes.punchline: Ray lists increasingly specific reasons why Johnny failed the shoot, continuing to enumerate them until Johnny cuts him off.
jokes.explanation: Ray's oblivious list of Johnny's photographic failures is a good escalation. 'Your face looked like it tasted something sour' is a specific and funny image. The 'or maybe...' implying there are even more reasons before Johnny cuts it off is good structural comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I could start a fire.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, no.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A small one.

jokes.text: Okay, Stevie, you need more confidence, because this is all you. / Like literally anything.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to pump up Stevie for a solo photo shoot, asking her to give something — anything — for the camera.
jokes.punchline: After a pause (presumably showing Stevie giving nothing), Alexis' defeated 'Like literally anything' — any expression at all would be progress.
jokes.explanation: The gap between Alexis' pep talk and Stevie's flat refusal to cooperate with the camera is a good deadpan visual moment. 'Like literally anything' as a defeated concession is funny and very Stevie-as-immovable-object.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I knew it! I could tell from the shape of Jocelyn's glow. That's a boy glow. I have an eye for these things.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Didn't you say earlier you thought it was a girl?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I said I *considered* it might be a girl. But my intuition, which is never wrong, told me boy.

jokes.text: Okay, I'm gonna need a minute. / We didn't budget for that, but sure.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks for a moment to compose herself for the photo.
jokes.punchline: Alexis treats a one-minute break as a budget item — 'we didn't budget for that' — as if this were a professional film production.
jokes.explanation: 'We didn't budget for that, but sure' is a great Alexis line — treating a free photo shoot as a professional production with a budget. The self-seriousness is the joke. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The name is rather stunted, isn't it? 'The Rosebud Motel.' It sounds like a place where people go to die.

jokes.text: Attention everyone, after much, much discussion, and a brief seance, Roland and I have decided...
jokes.setup: Moira gathers the town hall group to announce a decision about the baby secret.
jokes.punchline: 'And a brief seance' — casually dropped into the list of consultations as if it's a normal deliberative step.
jokes.explanation: The callback to witchcraft (Salem, 'stupid witch') now elevated to an actual seance is perfectly Moira. 'A brief seance' delivered as a casual procedural step — alongside 'much, much discussion' — is absurdly funny. The character integration is near-perfect: only Moira would hold a seance for a baby gender reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, Budd has two d's? That looks so wrong.

jokes.text: Uh, Gwen has stopped her Pilates tape for this, so uh, can we get a move on here?
jokes.setup: The group is stalling on revealing the baby's sex while Gwen waits on the phone.
jokes.punchline: The detail that Gwen has paused her Pilates tape — as if that's the relevant urgency — is a gloriously specific Roland touch. The Pilates tape (not video, not class — tape) also implies vintage media.
jokes.explanation: 'Pilates tape' is a perfect Roland-world detail — the urgency of Gwen's fitness VHS is treated as a legitimate scheduling constraint. Character-specific and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not trying to brag, but I got a B-minus on my motel rebranding project.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Which, if you know anything about grades, is basically an A.

jokes.text: It's a girl. / Tammy! / Or a boy. / Huh? / Perhaps it's a boy. / Wait, which one is it?
jokes.setup: Moira finally reveals the baby's sex — 'It's a girl!' Jocelyn explodes with 'Tammy!' (presumably the name they had for a girl).
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately backtracks to 'perhaps it's a boy' — she's not actually sure. The reveal reveal becomes an un-reveal.
jokes.explanation: The structural reversal — the long-awaited reveal immediately taken back — is a satisfying comedic subversion. Jocelyn's immediate 'Tammy!' (she had a name ready) adds a layer of pathos/comedy to the reversal. Moira not actually knowing is a good escalation of her earlier near-slip.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: 77%? That's barely a C.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, not everyone can be as academically gifted as you were in high school.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I got a 94 in calculus.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, and we've all heard about it. Many times. We know.

jokes.text: All of a sudden, expected attention has me doubting what I saw. I barely glanced at that paper.
jokes.setup: Everyone is staring at Moira waiting for the definitive answer.
jokes.punchline: Moira admits under pressure that she actually barely glanced at the paper — her knowledge was always shaky. 'Expected attention' as the destabilizing factor is a wonderfully Moira way to say 'I'm not sure.'
jokes.explanation: 'Expected attention has me doubting what I saw' is vintage Moira — theatrical self-drama, grammatically unusual, and oddly specific. The reveal that she barely glanced reframes her entire confident tenure as Secret-Keeper.

jokes.text: No. No, I very clearly remember, an M and an F. Just can't recall which one was circled.
jokes.setup: Moira tries to recover by claiming she does remember the paper.
jokes.punchline: She remembers there was an M and an F — i.e., the literal options — but not which one was marked. This is not information.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: Moira claims to 'very clearly remember,' then the specificity of what she remembers — just the two options, not the answer — is magnificently useless. 'Just can't recall which one was circled' delivered with complete dignity is peak Moira. Economy is excellent, and the character integration is perfect: only Moira could fail this specifically while maintaining composure.

jokes.text: It's your fault! / You told me to keep it a secret, and that's exactly what I did, even from myself.
jokes.setup: Roland accuses Moira of not knowing. Moira defends herself.
jokes.punchline: 'That's exactly what I did, even from myself.' — Moira's secret-keeping was so thorough she kept it from her own memory. An absurd but internally consistent defense.
jokes.explanation: A top-tier Moira line. The logic is absurd but delivered with complete conviction — she SECRET-KEPT the information even from herself. This is comedically sophisticated: it takes a real psychological concept (compartmentalization) and pushes it to absurdist extreme while staying in character. Highly quotable and very rewatchable.

jokes.text: Where did you put the sonogram? / In the safe. / And I wrote down the new combination on a sticky note. / Perfect, so where's the sticky note? / Well, the sticky note, I believe, is also in the safe.
jokes.setup: They need the sonogram to settle the question. Roland put it in the safe. He wrote the combination on a sticky note.
jokes.punchline: The sticky note is also in the safe — a perfect closed loop of incompetence.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural callback to the episode's opening safe joke. The escalation — solution locked inside the problem — is a classically structured comedy loop. 'Well, the sticky note, I believe, is also in the safe' delivered with Roland's characteristic sheepishness is perfect. Rewatch bonus because the cold open safe gag pays off here.

jokes.text: Well that, I will admit, is my blunder.
jokes.setup: Roland has locked the combination to the safe inside the safe.
jokes.punchline: Roland's gentle understatement — 'that is my blunder' — as if locating the error rather than acknowledging the chaos he's created.
jokes.explanation: 'That is my blunder' — the use of 'blunder' (mild, old-fashioned) for a complete system failure is funny in its understatement. Roland taking mild ownership is in-character.

jokes.text: Gwen, you still there? Hello? I'm goin' home.
jokes.setup: Gwen has been waiting on the phone through this entire debacle.
jokes.punchline: Gwen's silent hang-up is the correct response. The joke is her dignified exit from an absurd situation she can no longer justify staying for.
jokes.explanation: Gwen's 'I'm goin' home' as the punchline on the entire safe saga is economical and correct. She's been waiting patiently through everything and this is the last straw.

jokes.text: Gwen, you still there? Hello? / I'm goin' home.
jokes.setup: The whole group has been on the phone with Gwen throughout this increasingly absurd situation.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie, not Gwen, announces she's going home — she's had enough. Presumably Gwen has already hung up.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the misfire — Roland calls for Gwen, and Ronnie answers instead, equally done with the situation. 'I'm goin' home' from Ronnie is perfect character economy. The ambiguity about whether Gwen is also gone adds a layer.

jokes.text: I'm playing around with Ray, we took some shots with my hands outta my pockets, and he thinks with a little Photoshopping, they may turn out okay.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to reassure Stevie by reporting progress on the Johnny photos.
jokes.punchline: The best-case scenario for Johnny's photos is that they 'may turn out okay' with Photoshop — damning with faint praise and implying they're currently terrible.
jokes.explanation: A mild supporting laugh. The faint praise of Johnny's photos is funny but not standout. Functional scene-work.

jokes.text: Okay, Stevie, as VP, and CEO of In-House publicity, growing the business is generally considered to be a good thing.
jokes.setup: Stevie expresses ambivalence about growing the business.
jokes.punchline: Alexis has now promoted herself from 'VP of Marketing' to also 'CEO of In-House publicity' — a title she's apparently just invented mid-conversation.
jokes.explanation: A mild callback to Alexis' title-inflation pattern. Adding 'CEO of In-House publicity' to her already invented VP title is funny as escalation, though not as sharp as the original 'brand invigorator' gag.

jokes.text: Johnny's looking half-normal.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie are having a genuine moment of connection that Ray is photographing.
jokes.punchline: Alexis' commentary on the improved photos — 'Johnny's looking half-normal' — is the best she can say.
jokes.explanation: 'Half-normal' as the ceiling of Johnny's photogenicity is funny — it's simultaneously a compliment and an insult, delivered with Alexis' typical obliviousness. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: Oh dear, you're awfully brave allowing your beau to indulge himself like this. / Okay, this was not my idea.
jokes.setup: Moira approaches David as Patrick prepares to perform.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames Patrick performing as David 'allowing' it — implying David is being selflessly permissive. David clarifies he had no choice.
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical framing of David's suffering as bravery is in-character. 'This was not my idea' as David's response confirms his ongoing victimhood in this situation.

jokes.text: I would like to dedicate this song to a very special someone in my life. / David Rose. — Okay. / There he is, right there. That's him. Can't miss him.
jokes.setup: Patrick dedicates his song to someone special, building anticipation.
jokes.punchline: He names David, then points him out — 'There he is, right there. That's him. Can't miss him' — as David presumably tries to disappear into the crowd.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's cheerful spotlighting of David — 'Can't miss him' — while David presumably wishes the floor would swallow him is a warm cringe comedy beat. The dedication itself is a sweet moment that works comedically because of David's mortification. Rewatch bonus because the emotional payoff (it IS sweet) changes the comedy texture.

jokes.text: I would like to dedicate this song to a very special someone in my life. David Rose. There he is, right there. That's him. Can't miss him.
jokes.setup: Patrick announces he's dedicating the song to someone special.
jokes.punchline: He names David, then physically identifies him in the crowd — 'There he is, right there. That's him. Can't miss him.' — in a way that's simultaneously sweet and mortifying for David.
jokes.explanation: The escalating specificity of 'There he is, right there. That's him. Can't miss him.' transforms a romantic gesture into an embarrassing spotlight. Patrick is clearly enjoying David's discomfort while also being genuinely sincere — a perfect character beat for their relationship.

jokes.text: You'll let me know if I should pull the fire alarm? / Yes.
jokes.setup: David is bracing himself for Patrick's performance.
jokes.punchline: David's fire alarm escape plan — delivered dead-seriously — and Patrick's unconcerned 'Yes' agreement.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's nonchalant 'Yes' to the fire alarm plan — agreeing that this is a reasonable emergency protocol — is the better half of this joke. His confidence is undisturbed. Good economy, very quotable.

jokes.text: Moira? Oh, you two, I told you I don't know. But we do. We called the doctor. You did? Lovely. Don't you wanna know what it is? I'll give you three guesses.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn interrupt during Patrick's performance to tell Moira they've learned the baby's sex.
jokes.punchline: When asked if she wants to know, Moira says 'I'll give you three guesses' — revealing she actually does know after all, or is bluffing, or doesn't care — the ambiguity is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'I'll give you three guesses' is a Moira deflection — she's more interested in the game of knowing than the answer itself. Her theatrical nonchalance is consistent and funny.

jokes.text: Moira, we're having a boy. / A boy, I believe I told you that. / How wonderful!
jokes.setup: Roland confirms via the doctor that it's a boy — resolution after Moira's complete failure as a secret-keeper.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately claims she told them it was a boy — revising history — and her 'How wonderful!' pivot to joy is instantaneous.
jokes.explanation: Moira's immediate revisionism — 'I believe I told you that' after spectacularly failing to know — is perfect character comedy. The audacity and the instant pivot to celebration captures Moira's defining trait: she rewrites reality in real time. Very strong character integration.

jokes.text: My boy right now is being serenaded by his butter-voiced beau.
jokes.setup: Moira has just learned she's having a grandson (Roland's baby, not Moira's). She comments on the open mic performance.
jokes.punchline: 'Butter-voiced beau' — Moira's specific, theatrical compliment for Patrick performing 'Simply the Best.'
jokes.explanation: 'Butter-voiced beau' is an exceptional Moira coinage — alliterative, theatrical, specific, and weirdly perfect. The phrase is both a compliment and Moira-speak for 'nice voice.' High quotability because it's the kind of phrase only Moira would invent.

jokes.text: This would've been much more of a surprise, if I hadn't heard the construction all morning.
jokes.setup: Johnny is about to reveal the new motel sign to Stevie, who's blindfolded.
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately deflates the theatrical reveal by noting she heard the construction all morning — she already knows a sign went up.
jokes.explanation: A sharp Stevie line that instantly deflates Johnny's big moment. The practicality — she works there, of course she heard it — is pure Stevie. The gap between Johnny's theatrical 'surprise' and Stevie's blunt awareness is very funny.

jokes.text: Why isn't it just Rose Motel? / The Rosebud makes it a sad stunted thing.
jokes.setup: Stevie questions the 'Rosebud' name. Moira explains her objection.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'The Rosebud makes it a sad stunted thing' — a reference to the Citizen Kane meaning of rosebud (lost childhood/innocence) delivered as aesthetic criticism of a motel sign.
jokes.explanation: Moira applying Citizen Kane analysis to a motel sign name is perfectly in-character. 'A sad stunted thing' is a great phrase, and the fact that she's aesthetically correct about the cultural baggage while everyone else is just happy about the sign is very Moira.

jokes.text: Yeah, except it's Budd, with two d's, but... / Okay, well that would just look insane.
jokes.setup: Johnny notes that Stevie's last name is 'Budd' with two d's — meaning the sign technically misspells it.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'that would just look insane' — Rosebudd with two d's is aesthetically unacceptable and they've already moved on.
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of 'Rosebudd' — just one extra 'd' making a motel sign look ridiculous — is a good payoff. Moira's immediate, decisive 'that would just look insane' dismisses Stevie's naming rights without a second thought. Good economy and callback structure.

jokes.text: Also, I didn't know that.
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals Stevie's last name is Budd with two d's — a fact Alexis apparently didn't know despite designing the entire rebrand around it.
jokes.punchline: Alexis' breezy 'Also, I didn't know that' — as if it's a minor footnote that she didn't know the last name of the person she was rebranding.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect final joke. 'Also, I didn't know that' — the casual 'also' implying this is just one of several things she didn't know, delivered without shame — is very Alexis. The economy is exceptional: five words revealing she built an entire marketing strategy around a person whose last name she didn't know. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Can we have breakfast now? / Yeah, let's celebrate. Come on! / Oh, um, not to brag, but this re-brand got me 77% on my final marketing exam.
jokes.setup: The group looks at the new sign in a celebratory moment.
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually mentions she used this rebrand for her marketing exam and got 77% — simultaneously bragging and revealing she's currently a student doing homework on their business.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Alexis' 'pro bono' professional rebrand was actually her homework — and she got a C+ — is a great character moment. 'Not to brag' before a 77% is perfect Alexis self-awareness-free confidence.

jokes.text: Okay, have you ever gotten an A? / Enough, David.
jokes.setup: Alexis brags about her 77% as impressive.
jokes.punchline: David asks if she's ever gotten an A (implying she hasn't). Johnny shuts it down — 'Enough, David.'
jokes.explanation: David's dig is predictable but lands because of the delivery. Johnny's 'Enough, David' as the episode's closing line is a good button — warm dad shutting down sibling bickering as a final note.

tmdb_overview: David and Patrick host an open mic night; Johnny and Stevie start rebranding the hotel.
insight: Character comedy drives 51 jokes across open mic chaos, but pacing falters at 2.2 per minute.
